flashcards.Back: The protocol (Grey 28) mandates that emergency vehicle operators must drive with due regard for the safety of all persons.
A legal standard requiring emergency drivers to operate safely and predictably, regardless of sirens or lights.
A standardized management tool for organizing resources and personnel during an emergency incident.
Under ICS, the ideal ratio of supervisors to subordinates is 1 to 5 to maintain effective management.
Includes the Safety Officer, Liaison Officer, and Public Information Officer who report directly to the Incident Commander.
Consists of the Operations, Planning, Logistics, and Finance/Administration sections.
Responsible for managing all tactical operations and carrying out the Incident Action Plan.
Collects, evaluates, and disseminates incident situation information and maintains resource status.
Provides facilities, services, and material support (food, fuel, supplies) for the incident.
Tracks incident costs, personnel time, and handles procurement and compensation.
Assessment criteria for adult triage: Respirations, Perfusion, and Mental Status.
Patients with life-threatening injuries who require immediate care (e.g., airway blockage, severe bleeding).
Patients with serious but non-life-threatening injuries who can wait for treatment (e.g., stable fractures).
Often called the 'walking wounded,' these patients have minor injuries and are the last priority.
Patients who are already dead or have injuries so catastrophic that survival is not possible.
A respiratory rate over 30 breaths per minute indicates an 'Immediate' (Red) priority.
A capillary refill time greater than 2 seconds indicates an 'Immediate' (Red) priority.
Failure to follow simple commands classifies a patient as 'Immediate' (Red) priority.
A specialized triage system for pediatric patients that accounts for different normal physiological ranges.
The safe distance maintained between an ambulance and other vehicles to provide adequate reaction time.
A psychological state where the siren sound causes the driver to increase speed and take unnecessary risks.
The contaminated area immediately surrounding a hazardous materials release; requires specialized protective gear.
The transition area where decontamination procedures for personnel and equipment take place.
The safe area where the command post and other support functions are located; no contamination allowed.
Direct permission given by a conscious, competent adult for medical care and transport.
The legal assumption that an unconscious or incapacitated patient would want life-saving medical treatment.
Requires four components: Duty to act, Breach of duty, Damages, and Causation.
Termination of care without the patient's consent or without transferring to a provider of equal/higher skill.
To protect the privacy and security of a patient's protected health information (PHI).
The level of care expected from a reasonable person with similar training under similar circumstances.
The legal boundaries defining what an EMT is permitted to do according to state law and medical oversight.
The process of verifying fuel levels, medical supplies, and equipment functionality before the shift starts.
The use of chemical agents to kill microorganisms on non-critical surfaces after patient contact.
An ideal helicopter landing area should measure 100 feet by 100 feet on level ground.
Always approach from the front (10 to 2 o'clock positions) and only after being signaled by the pilot.
Gaining entry to a vehicle or structure without using tools (e.g., opening an unlocked door).
Gaining entry to a vehicle or structure using specialized tools (e.g., glass breakers or cutters).
An incident where the number of patients exceeds the immediate resources available to manage them.
A reference manual for first responders to identify hazards and safety distances at a Hazmat scene.
A document providing detailed info on a chemical's properties, hazards, and emergency first-aid steps.
Provide a brief 'windshield survey' size-up, establish command, and name the incident.
Allows agencies with different legal/functional responsibilities to work together for common objectives.
Serves as the primary point of contact for assisting and cooperating outside agencies.
The Maine protocol defining criteria for when EMS may stop CPR efforts in the field.
A legal obligation to provide care once the EMT is dispatched or arrives on the scene while working.
Use plain English, keep messages brief, and avoid using patient names over the air.
The primary legal and medical documentation of the entire patient encounter and EMS actions.
A formal session held within 24-72 hours of a major event to help responders cope with stress.
The use of chemicals to kill all microorganisms except bacterial spores; used for items like laryngoscope blades.
Maine protocol emphasizing patient and crew safety through proper restraint and driving precautions.
